117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.

145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.

5772	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
5773	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules

5776	  \]

5782	\begin{proof}
5783	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv,
5784	        lem:slice_dual_transport}
5785	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5786	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5787	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5788	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5789	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5790	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5791	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5792	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5793	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5794	
5795	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5796	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
5797	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
5798	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
5799	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
5800	  \[
5801	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
5802	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
5803	  \]
5804	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
5805	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
5806	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
5807	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
5808	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
5809	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
5810	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
5811	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
5812	
5813	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
5814	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
5815	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
5816	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
5817	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
5818	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left

5820	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
5821	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
5822	  \emph{exactly}

5824	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
5825	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5826	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
5827	  \]

5829	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
5830	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
5831	  two-leg split of the whole step.
5832	
5833	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
5834	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5835	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
5836	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain

5841	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set

5843	
5844	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5845	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5846	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5847	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5848	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5849	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5850	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5851	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5852	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5853	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
5854	
5855	  \emph{The combined leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) atom \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\).} Both legs are
5856	  realised together by a single \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism

5858	  \emph{by hand} --- it is \emph{not} obtained by consuming a sheaf-level slice equivalence.
5859	  This is essential: the equivalence of \emph{sheaves of modules} over an open immersion

5862	  reindexing along the open-immersion direct-image functor \(f_{!}\) on opens

5869	  \(\mathtt{sliceDualTransport}\,f\,M\,V\) at \(W \le V\) is the categorical image
5870	  \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\,(\varphi_{\bullet})\) of the dual-section

5872	  \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) by restriction of scalars along \(\beta_W\), post-composed with
5873	  the leg-(B) codomain swap \(\mathtt{inv}(\varepsilon(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W))\).
5874	  Leg (A) is built categorically via \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\beta_W).\mathrm{map}\):
5875	  a genuine change-of-rings functor acts here, so the reindexing must be applied as a
5876	  functor map rather than pointwise. Leg (B)'s unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) is invertible
5877	  because \(\beta_W\) is a ring isomorphism, as detailed in \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport}.
5878	  Because \(\mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) is a thin poset, the
5879	  naturality squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). The forward and inverse maps
5880	  identify the slice-Hom value over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV)\) with the slice-Hom value over
5881	  \(\mathtt{Over}\,V\), \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linearly.

5887	  \cref{lem:slice_dual_transport} carries the full leg-(A)\(\circ\)(B) content.

5889	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is

5896	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5897	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For

5899	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single

5909	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
